title: Holiday Potluck
description:
Join R-ladies for a potluck! Please bring a dish (no drinks to respect Optimism's business) to share and come socialize and hang out with other R-ladies.

Please bring any serving utensils needed for your dish; we will have paper plates and silverware.

title: Use Cases and Careers: What folks are doing with R at work!
description:
Come hear about how people are using R at work, careers in R, and more!

Schedule:
6:30-6:45pm: Welcome and Socializing
6:45-7:30pm: Careers and Use Cases Talks by Kelly Mistry and Marie Jordan
7:30-8:00pm: Conference connections, potluck planning, bonus networking time

You are welcome to bring babies and kids along. We just ask that you try to minimize any disruptions to the group.

Thank you to Insight Data Science for hosting and sponsoring our event!

title: Less-Traditional Uses of R and More!
description:
Come hear about some non-traditional uses of R, see demos of a couple shiny apps, and more! Plus, there will be pizza.

Schedule:
6:30-6:45pm: Welcome and Socializing
6:45-7:30pm: Liz Kellogg: Project Management Using R
7:30-8pm: Lightning Talks
Kara Woo: Tracking Gas Mileage
Megsie Siple: Shiny Tips and Tricks
Pamela Moriarty: History of R

You are welcome to bring babies and kids along. We just ask that you try to minimize any disruptions to the group.

Thank you to Insight Data Science for hosting and sponsoring our event!

title: Data Science Portfolios
description: Learn the do's and don'ts of putting together a data science portfolio with Rachael. We'll be meeting in the Google Fremont office & due to security we do need to get all RSVPs by Friday morning.

title: August R-Ladies: Problem-solving with R packages, RStudio add-ins
description:
Kerice Doten-Snitker will be giving a talk on some problem-solving she's done using the riverdist package off-label and Rebecca Ferrell will be giving a talk on quality-of-life improving RStudio add-ins.

The meetup is generously being sponsored by Convoy!
Please be aware that the building doors automatically lock at 6pm, so we will have people to let you in and direct you to Convoy's offices.

You are welcome to bring your kids along as needed, please just try to limit disruptions to the group.

title: July R-Ladies: Blogdown and Networking!
description:
Isabella Velasquez will be giving a talk on how to create a website using blogdown. We'll follow that with a networking / unconference breakout session - come see us on Slack to vote on breakout topics.

The meetup is generously being sponsored by Convoy!
Please be aware that the building doors automatically lock at 6pm, so we will have people to let you in and direct you to Convoy's offices.

title: Workflow and Reproducibility in R
description:
Git is a commonly used tool that is great for improving workflow. Chaya Jones will be giving an intro to git, focused on its integration with Rstudio. A newer tool, Drake, aims to improve aspects of workflow and work reproducibility. Christine Stawitz will be giving an introduction to using Drake.

The meetup is generously being sponsored by Convoy!
Please be aware that the building doors automatically lock at 6pm, so we will have people to let you in and direct you to Convoy's offices.

title: Blogging and Contributing to R Projects
description: This month we've got an international visitor from Barcelona! Come hear R-Ladies Global Social Master Maëlle Salmon talk about blog content and promotion. In addition, Kara Woo will be giving a practice talk for her keynote talk at CascadiaR on making contributions to established R projects. Help out a fellow R-Lady and give feedback and suggestions to make her talk extra awesome!

venues.name: eScience Institute
venues.address: WRF Data Science Studio, UW Physics/Astronomy Tower, 6th Floor, University of Washington

title: Happy Hour!
description: First Friday of the Month = Happy Hour! Come hang out with your fellow R-Ladies. Both R related and non r-related chatter are equally welcome!

title: Spatial Data Visualizations
description:
Aysegul Kramer will present some of her current work visualizing spatial data. Aysegul is looking for some feedback and suggestions, so come prepared to help a fellow R-Lady out!

Afterwards there will be time to bring your own project to work on. You are welcome to work independently or ask for help from your fellow R-Ladies!

venues.name: eScience Institute
venues.address: WRF Data Science Studio, UW Physics/Astronomy Tower, 6th Floor, University of Washington

title: Happy Hour!
description: First Friday of the Month = Happy Hour! Come hang out with your fellow R-Ladies. Both R related and non r-related chatter are equally welcome!

title: Intro to Shiny and Web API Data
description:
• What we'll do
Intro to Accessing Web API Data- Nina Cesare
Intro to Shiny - Bethany Yollin

Lightning Talks:
How to Give a Lightning Talk - Rachael Tatman
Solving a Polyalphabetic Cypher - Nissa Ferm

• What to bring
The web API data will be an interactive demo, so please bring a laptop if you would like to follow along.

venues.name: eScience Institute
venues.address: WRF Data Science Studio, UW Physics/Astronomy Tower, 6th Floor, University of Washington

title: Happy Hour with R-Ladies Taipei
description:
We have some special guests in town from R-Ladies Taipei: https://blogs.msdn.microsoft.com/mvpawardprogram/2018/02/15/qa-chiayi-yen/

This event will be a very informal happy hour, with some opportunities for show-and-tell. Bring your laptops and your interesting projects if you have any to show off, or just come to network and hang out with R-Ladies colleagues from around the globe.

Save the date for our next presentation-style meetup: March 19.

title: Kick-Off Meet and Greet!
description:
Welcome to our kick-off meeting of the Seattle R-Ladies chapter. Our first meetup will have a brief intro to R-Ladies Global and the mission of the organization, and then we'll open it up for questions, brainstorming topics for future sessions, and have time for chatting, networking, and getting to know each other. We'll have some snacks to share and some friendly faces!

If you won't be able to make the first meetup but want to let us know who you are and what you're interested in, drop us a line and we can send you our new-chapter survey.

• Important things to know:
R-Ladies code of conduct: https://github.com/rladies/starter-kit/wiki/Code-of-Conduct

venues.name: eScience Institute
venues.address: WRF Data Science Studio, UW Physics/Astronomy Tower, 6th Floor, University of Washington

title: R-Ladies 2019 Hot Chocolate Happy Hour
description:
Come celebrate the end of the year with a hot chocolate happy hour at Kakao! ☕✨

📅 Year in Review
Let's explore what we covered this year, what awesome tools we learned about, and what amazing things our fellow R-Ladies have accomplished!

👯 Organizer updates
A few updates for next year, and a call for those who are interested to join us as organizers! It's a wonderful, fufilling experience.

💡 Ideas for next year
Is there an R/data science topic you wish you knew more about? Or something super cool that you did that you want to share? Let us know! We're always welcoming new ideas.

⬢ Hex Competition voting
Throughout the month of November, we are holding a hex design competition. Represent our city on a hex for all to see! Please submit your entries here! http://bit.ly/seahex

Thank you for an amazing 2019 and we look forward to having fun with R together in 2020! 💜

title: Project-oriented workflows in R
description:
Kara Woo will present on project-oriented workflows in R. The workshop will cover the first two modules of the two-day What They Forgot to Teach You About R workshop (https://rstudio-conf-2020.github.io/what-they-forgot/). This workshop is designed for experienced R and RStudio users who want to (re)design their R lifestyle. You'll learn holistic workflows that address the most common sources of friction in data analysis.

You should take this workshop if: you've been using R for a while and you feel like writing R code is not what's holding you back the most. You've realized that you have more pressing "meta" problems that no one seems to talk about: You want to know how to divide your work into projects and scripts and how to share your work with others.

venues.name: eScience Institute
venues.address: WRF Data Science Studio, UW Physics/Astronomy Tower, 6th Floor, University of Washington

title: Spooky data stories
description:
Data formatting hell? Machine learning horror? We’re looking for 5-10 minute presentations about your scariest data stories and how you solved them using R.

We would love for you to be a speaker! Please sign up here: https://forms.gle/xaNbcku6PAnLRuQ19

For a wonderful reference from a previous speaker, please see Rachael Tatman's deck on How to Prepare & Give A ⚡Lightning⚡ Talk:
https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1nod6zgs1DQczpxWg_8opKS7gDmzWMmjMNWIxKzjhEWM/edit#slide=id.p

Many thanks to Sage Bionetworks for hosting us and providing pizza!

Thank you and see you soon!

title: Happy Hour Hangout
description:
Join other R-ladies for a low key social event! Chatting about R and non-R topics are equally welcome! :)

Warning: Happy hour prices at Cactus actually end at 6pm, so feel free to show up a few minutes early to grab a discounted snack or drink. Organizers may just not be around until 6.

title: Code Reviews! Learn how to review code & submit code for review
description:
How do you review code from a data science project? Often, the goal of code review is to ensure that new code is free of bugs, efficient, well-documented, and maintainable. Code review is also an excellent opportunity to learn from others and get feedback on your work. However, practices vary widely from academia to industry and standards are mostly non-existent.

At this event, we will hear from R-Ladies members about best practices for code review, how code review is conducted in their workplace, and then we'll practice code review in small groups.

Speakers:
Lauren Wolfe, Research Data Specialist at Fred Hutch
Carrie Bennette, Principal Quantitative Scientist at Flatiron

We are looking for R-Ladies members to share code that they'd like feedback on for the group exercise. Please comment in the event discussion thread if you have code you'd like to bring to the meeting.

Pizza & refreshments will be provided by Fred Hutch.
Please feel free to bring children if childcare is unavailable.

title: Seattle Summer Lightning Talks!
description:
Is it too sunny out? Are you missing the Seattle rain? Get your fix at an R-Ladies Lightning Talk Session! Lightning talks are informal, 5 minute talks on anything R, data science, or tech related. Examples are:

* Your favorite R learning resource
* How you use R at work
* Your favorite R package

We would love for you to be a speaker! Please sign up here: https://forms.gle/ZevMUKrNJLWdhkEu5

For a wonderful reference from a previous speaker, please see Rachael Tatman's deck on How to Prepare & Give A ⚡Lightning⚡ Talk:
https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1nod6zgs1DQczpxWg_8opKS7gDmzWMmjMNWIxKzjhEWM/edit#slide=id.p

Many thanks to Sage Bionetworks for hosting us!

Thank you and see you soon!

title: R-Ladies Social in SLU
description: Join us for an R-Ladies social event at Brave Horse Tavern in South Lake Union. Connect and catch up with other R-Ladies members. We've planned out some great events for the rest of 2019, which we'll review at the social, and we'd love to hear your thoughts on future events.

title: The Ministry of Silly Talks + SuperMeetup Social Hour
description:
*RSVP HERE: https://www.meetup.com/Seattle-PyLadies/events/261516172/ Centralized RSVPs help the organizers plan, thank you for helping us!*

Lots of folks in town for SSDS in Bellevue from 29 May - 1 June. Lovely time for a larger get together!

5:00PM - 6:00PM - Social Hour w/ Food

6:00PM - 7:00PM - The Ministry of Silly Talks!

Please sign up to give a 5-minute lightning talk on whatever you want (non-technical talks welcome!) here: http://bit.ly/pyladies-ministry-of-silly-talks

7:00PM - 8:00PM - More Social Hour (optional speed networking round)

title: Learning R and Building Community
description:
Mark your calendars! This month we will hear from 2 people about their pathways to learning R and the great community-building that happened along the way. Redfin has kindly offered to host us in their space in downtown Seattle and will be providing pizza.

Speakers:
* Stefanie Butland, Community Manager at rOpenSci
* Malisa Smith, Bioinformatician at University of Washington

Stefanie Butland:
I am the Community Manager for an organization of R practitioners...but I am not an R practitioner. For the first 1.5 years in my job, this was a feature, but lately it has felt more like a bug. In this talk I will introduce rOpenSci, our packages, and our system for transparent software peer review, and then share my story of starting to learn R by (not so) passive absorption of information and best practices from R-Ladies, rOpenSci, and the broader R world.

Malisa Smith:
Flow cytometry is commonly used by biologists to measure the abundance of specific molecules in millions of cells, while retaining information at the single-cell level. The resulting high-dimensional, high-throughput datasets have led to the development of unique software for analyzing flow cytometry data. I will talk about how I learned to use open source R packages for analyzing flow data, and I hope that my experience will help other people navigate the landscape of flow cytometry analysis tools and online software resources in general.

title: Transition to Data Science
description:
Join PyLadies, R-Ladies, Women in Data Science (WiDS), and Insight Data Science to learn about transitioning to a career in data science and the variety of roles that full under the title 'data scientist'!

To help us plan, please RSVP directly on the WiDS meetup page:
http://meetu.ps/e/GBzH9/GRKLB/f

We'll start with lightning talks from panelists on what their job as a data scientist entails, which will be followed by a panel discussion focused on how the panelists moved into the field and their experiences in it. There will, of course, be plenty of time for questions from attendees!

Panelists include:
- Lisa Qian
Data Science Manager at Airbnb, transitioned to DS 6 yrs ago
https://www.linkedin.com/in/lisa-qian-98b04326/
- Annette Taberner-Miller
Data Science Manager at Indeed, transitioned to DS 8 yrs ago
https://www.linkedin.com/in/annette-taberner-miller/
- Susan Fung
Sr. Data Analyst at Liberty Mutual, transitioned to DS 1 yrs ago
https://www.linkedin.com/in/susanfung/
- Nupur Mehta
Data Scientist II at Redfin, transitioned to DS 2 yrs ago
https://www.linkedin.com/in/nupurmehta4/
- Gina Schmalzle
Senior Data Scientist at Nordstrom, transitioned to DS 4 yrs ago
https://www.linkedin.com/in/ginaschmalzle/
- Heather Nolis
Machine Learning Engineer at T-Mobile, transitioned to DS 1 yr ago
https://www.linkedin.com/in/heatherwensler/

Many thanks to Insight Data Science for sponsoring this event!

Schedule:
6:15-6:45: arrive, pizza, drinks
6:45-7:15: lightning talks on what data scientist jobs can include
7:15-8: panel
8-8:30: networking

title: Women in Computing: History and Present
description:
Come join us at our March R-Ladies Meetup!

This month, we'll be celebrating and talking about the trans*, nonbinary, agender, genderqueer and female people who have gone before us and inspired us with their programming prowess.

From Ada Lovelace to current mentors, we encourage you to bring fun stories, book recommendations, advice for mentors and mentees, and your own awesome accomplishments. (Stories and experiences of people of color are especially encouraged!) We'll be breaking out into small groups to share these stories and see where the conversation goes. Please don't feel you have to bring a story to participate; these will be loose, fun, and adaptable groups!

The meeting will wind up with an rstudio::conf watch party, the lecture TBD. Please feel free to reach out if there's a particular topic you're interested in.

Watch this space for further details as we get closer to the date.

title: Intro to Shiny Workshop
description:
Join us for a hands-on workshop that will introduce you to building a web app in R using Shiny taught by Megsie Siple.

Please bring a laptop with Rstudio. Unfortunately, we do not have funding to provide lunch, so please bring lunch along or plan on purchasing it. There are plenty of nearby options and we will definitely have a lunch break!

We expect this workshop to fill and have a waitlist, as we are aware many people have been wanting to learn Shiny! To make sure that as many people are able to attend and benefit as possible, we ask that if you RSVP yes, you are definitively planning to come. If your plans change, please do update your RSVP.

Prereq: A basic knowledge of R. You absolutely do not need to be a high-powered R user to benefit from this workshop! If you have any questions or concerns, please feel free to send us a message. We are committed to making this accessible to as wide a range of R skills as possible.

If you have an app you know you'd like to build, feel free to bring code, data, or an outline of what you want to do. This workshop is open to anyone who wants to expand their Shiny skills, but will be targeted towards people who are just starting out.

venues.name: eScience Institute
venues.address: WRF Data Science Studio, UW Physics/Astronomy Tower, 6th Floor, University of Washington

title: Data Structures in R: Theory and Applications
description:
Please join us at our January meetup at Fred Hutch (Arnold Building) to learn all about data structures in R and beyond. We will host two speakers, who will give us a background in the basics of data structures in R, and then give us an intro to R's S4 class (often used in Bioconductor packages). This event will be aimed at R-Ladies at all levels of experience.

We will begin with socializing/networking at 6pm. Talks will begin at 6:30pm.

Pizza will be provided.
Please bring your laptop if you would like to participate in a coding exercise.

Speakers:
*new speaker*
Rachael Tatman is a data scientist at Kaggle with a linguistics PhD she occasionally dusts off and gets some use from. Her professional interests include natural language processing, statistics, fairness accountability and transparency in machine learning and, of course, R.

Chao-Jen Wong is a Bioinformatician at Tapscott Lab in Fred Hutch working on discovery of cure for Facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy. She is also an organizer of FredHutch Bioinformatics Interest Group (FHBIg.io). Prior to that, she was an R programmer and core member of the Bioconductor Project. She has a PhD in Applied Mathematics from Claremont Graduate University. When she is not working or studying, she enjoys cooking and hauling her kids around town with her electric bike.

title: Our Year in Data
description:
Join us for lunch time lightning talks exploring our favorite data visualizations of 2020. Sign up to present here (https://forms.gle/iANLN28nNvZCwV1q8).

Many of us participate in TidyTuesday, a weekly social data project in R (https://github.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday). Present your favorite project from this year's TidyTuesday's data sets, or another data set you found interesting in 2020.

If you want to learn more about TidyTuesday or get help with a project, we'll host "office hours" the week before on our R-Ladies Slack channel. Please message the meeting host if you need to be invited to the Slack channel.

Office hours: 12-1pm on December 8th and 9th.

title: R-Ladies Seattle and Allyship
description:
**Note that we've moved this event from July 16th at 6:30pm to August 5th at noon.**

After a 3-month hiatus, we'll be returning with a virtual event focused on allyship in the R-Ladies Seattle community. We will discuss how, as a community of R users, we can make changes to our group so that R-Ladies members who are Black, Indigenous, and POC will feel supported, included, and enthusiastically welcomed and celebrated. We will also use this event as a forum to educate ourselves about how to be anti-racist data scientists, touching on places where allies can be most effective.

We will discuss meetup event best practices for diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) and changes we are working on for R-Ladies Seattle. We'll also lead a discussion about which projects/changes R-Ladies members would like to prioritize. Your input and participation in this event is welcomed and appreciated.

In the meantime, if you'd like get caught up on DEI initiatives and discussions in the broader R community, I recommend reading and viewing the following:

* Heather Turner's talk at CelebRation 2020 (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BbpkKzz71EY)
* Introducing MiR: A Community of Underrepresented Minority Users of R (https://medium.com/@doritolay/introducing-mir-a-community-for-underrepresented-users-of-r-7560def7d861)
* Supporting diversity in the R community (upcoming useR talk to be live streamed on July 17th: https://user2020.r-project.org/program/agenda/)
* Discussion between Heather Turner and Jenny Bryan on how to increase diversity of R Core on the R-Ladies Global Slack (if you are on this slack, see discussions in #events_global, invite link to Slack here: https://rladies-community-slack.herokuapp.com/)

Please reach out to R-Ladies Seattle co-organizers if you have any questions, concerns, comments, or ideas about this event. You can send us a message on the R-Ladies Seattle Slack, message us on Twitter (@rladiesseattle) or send an email to seattle@rladies.org.

title: {remotes} Lightning Talks
description:
Break out of that isolation and join us (virtually) for lightning talks!

Lightning talks are informal, 5 minute talks on anything R, data science, or tech related. Examples are:

* Working remotely with R and R tools
* Earth Day related talks
* Other projects, workflows, visualizations you've been working on!

Try out your Zoom presentation skills and be a speaker! Please sign up here: https://forms.gle/qqmmGVBLqcerfZ676

For a wonderful reference from a previous speaker, please see Rachael Tatman's deck on How to Prepare & Give A ⚡Lightning⚡ Talk:
https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1nod6zgs1DQczpxWg_8opKS7gDmzWMmjMNWIxKzjhEWM/edit#slide=id.p

Many thanks to Ookla for hosting us! You will see the Zoom link once you sign up and we will send out a password the day of the event.

Thank you and see you soon!

title: pRedicaments in R: An R-Ladies Remote Rendezvous
description:
Join R-Ladies Seattle for our first remote meeting on debugging and getting help! Inspired by Jenny Bryan's keynote at rstudio::conf (link below), we will take a deeper dive into how to resolve error messages in R.

We have 2 wonderful speakers who will be presenting.

_Christine Stawitz_
* I'll discuss my process for sifting through the (often less-than-helpful) documentation that comes with R packages, including function reference, vignettes, and GitHub issue forums.
* I'm a contractor at ECS Federal in support of NOAA Fisheries' Office of Science and Technology. I work on improving coding, documentation, and sharing practices for the software NOAA and academic fisheries scientists use to manage marine resources.

_Katie Jolly_
* I'll talk about using the {reprex} package for creating reproducible examples. This package makes it easier to write clear, concise, and readable code questions that will get a more helpful response in return.
* I am a data analyst at Ookla. I am interested in spatial data, information design, and civic data projects.

Details:
* We will send out conference call information to attendees who have RSVP'd "Yes" to this event. The call will be taking place over zoom.
* Thank you to Ookla for helping us get set up with zoom!

Link to Jenny Bryan's talk in case you'd like to view it before our meeting (not required!):
https://resources.rstudio.com/rstudio-conf-2020/object-of-type-closure-is-not-subsettable-jenny-bryan

title: Hacknight at Kakao
description:
About:

A hacknight is a free-style coding environment, where a group of individuals come together to work on various projects in one space, with the purpose to ask one another questions, ranging from specific troubleshooting to conceptual. These projects can be group projects or individual.

Details:

Bring your code and laptop and get ready to enjoy some coffee/hot chocolate and a good working environment.

I will be working on making an R package from code I’ve written over the years. I will be following this tutorial, which anyone can follow step-by-step even without any specific code to add to a package. http://web.mit.edu/insong/www/pdf/rpackage_instructions.pdf

If you do not have a specific project but would like to join, you could start (or finish if you attended the Rladies meetup on Feb 18th) your Tidy Tuesday plots. Tidy Tuesday datasets can be found at https://github.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday and you can follow this vignette from the awesome ggpubr package to make some pretty plots quickly https://rpkgs.datanovia.com/ggpubr/index.html.

For more plotting ideas/vignettes, check out http://www.sthda.com/english/articles/24-ggpubr-publication-ready-plots and built-in datasets are listed here: http://www.sthda.com/english/wiki/r-built-in-data-sets.

title: Tidy Tuesday and Pizza at Fred Hutch
description:
Tidy Tuesday is a weekly social data project in R organized by the R4DS Online Learning Community. Join R-Ladies on Tuesday, February 18th to learn more about Tidy Tuesday and to work on this week's project.

From the Tidy Tuesday website:
"Every week we post a raw dataset, a chart or article related to that dataset, and ask you to explore the data. While the dataset will be 'tamed', it will not always be tidy! As such you might need to apply various R for Data Science techniques to wrangle the data into a true tidy format. The goal of TidyTuesday is to apply your R skills, get feedback, explore other’s work, and connect with the greater #RStats community! As such we encourage everyone of all skills to participate!"

More information and the data for each week is available on the Tidy Tuesday website (https://github.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday).

Details:
6pm - 8pm
Fred Hutch Sze Conference Room in the Thomas Building
(Note that this is a different building than past R-Ladies events. See map here for directions: https://www.fredhutch.org/content/dam/www/about-us/contact-us/FredHutch_Map_09_2019.pdf)
Pizza provided by Fred Hutch

title: R configuration and purrr
description:
Kara Woo will present a workshop on R configuration, maintaining R, and how to repeat yourself with purrr. The workshop will cover the 5th and 8th modules of the two-day What They Forgot to Teach You About R workshop (https://rstudio-conf-2020.github.io/what-they-forgot/).

This workshop is designed for experienced R and RStudio users who want to (re)design their R lifestyle. You should take this workshop if you’ve been using R for a while, you feel like writing R code is not what’s holding you back the most, and you want to learn more about your R installation.

This is a hands-on workshop, so please bring a laptop with a current version of R and RStudio installed. RSVPing helps us know how many people to expect, but is not required.

This event is NOT sponsored by The Seattle Public Library.

venues.name: University Branch - The Seattle Public Library

title: R/Pharma Recap
description:
R-Ladies Seattle shares what they learned and liked about R/Pharma 2021!

R/Pharma is a relatively small, scientifically & industry oriented, collegial conference focused on the use of R in the development of pharmaceuticals. The 2021 Conference ran Nov. 2-4th, with workshops the week before. Learn more about the conference here: https://rinpharma.com/event/rinpharma2021/

Join us to learn what we learned!

title: Developing R Packages for Yourself and Others
description:
Please join us for a presentation on creating R packages with guest speaker Reiko Okamoto. Thank you Reiko for bringing your R-Ladies Vancouver presentation to R-Ladies Seattle!

Event Description: R packages are a collection of functions, documentation, and sample data. Not only do they provide an excellent framework for organizing your code, but they also make it easier to share code with other R users. In this workshop, we’ll develop a simple R package together to gain an understanding of the key steps of package development. I’ll also reflect on my experiences creating an internal package for my team at the BC Ministry of Health. I hope to shed light on the lesser-known non-technical aspects of package development by discussing how to identify what tools your teammates need to succeed and how to encourage them to adopt your package. This workshop is geared towards (but not limited to) those who are new to developing packages and those who are thinking of creating one to share within their organization and beyond.

**This workshop assumes you know how to write a function in R and are familiar with the RStudio IDE. (See below for resources and required packages) ***

About the Speaker: Reiko is a data analyst with the Government of British Columbia. In her role, she creates various tools using R to support her team’s work in monitoring trends in population health and health inequalities in the province. When her computer is turned off, she splits her time as a recreational bassist, aspiring Star Baker, and occasional trainspotter.

General workshop information:

- Please expect a hands-on session in a friendly and laid-back atmosphere where we all encouraged to learn together.
- This workshop assumes you know how to write a function in R and are familiar with the RStudio IDE. Follow the links below for a refresher:
- Yuka Takemon's Base R functions tutorial: https://www.yukatakemon.com/post/rladies-functions/rladies-building-functions/

Software requirements:
- R (Free at https://cran.r-project.org/)
- RStudio (Free at https://www.rstudio.com/products/rstudio/download/)

Package requirements: (Install the following using
`install.packages("packageNameHere")` in your console)
- devtools
- roxygen2
- testthat
- knitr

If this is your first R-Ladies event, please take a moment to review our R-Ladies Global code of conduct: https://github.com/rladies/.github/blob/master/CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md

title: Intermediate git & GitHub lightning talks
description:
There will be approx. 5 lightning talks (5-8 minutes) on topics ranging from pull requests to GitHub actions to security with time for questions and discussion at the end.

There is no prerequisite knowledge for attendees; however, if you are not a git user you may find it helpful to look through the materials for our Git through April workshops ahead of time https://github.com/kdillmcfarland/2021-04-02-r-ladies-seattle-online/tree/gh-pages/_episodes/R-ladies.

title: Dimensionality Reduction Tutorial: PCA & UMAP
description:
Please join us for a lunchtime R-Ladies meetup with Anabel Ruggiero. Anabel will guide us through a tutorial on dimensionality reduction. Dimensionality reduction is a useful technique for data visualization and as an initial layer of a model. This makes it a powerful tool in a data scientist's arsenal.

Anabel will go over the basics of the math and algorithms behind PCA, and UMAP. Most of the time will be devoted to an R example implementing these algorithms.

-- About Anabel --

Anabel Ruggiero is an unaffiliated researcher whose interests lie in the intersection of applied mathematics and software engineering. She received her doctorate in Chemical Engineering at Oklahoma State University, where she did research in the field of computational biosystems. Her current project is NNDescent.cpp, a reimplementation of and iteration upon PyNNDescent. This project is an entry in The Billion-Scale Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search Challenge hosted by NeurIPS 2021.

LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/anabel-ruggiero-138372114/
Github: https://github.com/AnabelSMRuggiero

-- How to follow Anabel’s tutorial --

Please find the presentation and associated GitHub repo here. **If you want to follow the full tutorial, please fork the repo and be ready to run the code**:
https://github.com/AnabelSMRuggiero/Dimensionality-Reduction-Tutorial

This tutorial will require the uwot and ggplot2 packages to be installed in R.
uwot: https://github.com/jlmelville/uwot
ggplot2: https://github.com/tidyverse/ggplot2

If you want to watch the presentation and not run the code in real time, that is also fine.

Please reach out if you have any questions (email: seattle@rladies.org).

-- Additional resources --

Principal Component Analysis: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Principal_component_analysis
How UMAP Works: https://umap-learn.readthedocs.io/en/latest/how_umap_works.html
TSNE vs UMAP: https://towardsdatascience.com/tsne-vs-umap-global-structure-4d8045acba17
UMAP: Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection for Dimension Reduction: https://arxiv.org/abs/1802.03426
Matrix Computations: http://math.ecnu.edu.cn/~jypan/Teaching/books/2013%20Matrix%20Computations%204th.pdf

title: Practice talks for CascadiaRConf
description: R-Ladies Seattle will be hosting practice talks for CascadiaRConf! We are also happy to have practice talks for other upcoming conferences. If you are interested in presenting, please let us know in Slack or in the GitHub issue for this event https://github.com/rladies-seattle/rladies-seattle-organizing/issues/22.

title: git through April: Collaborating in GitHub
description:
Join us over lunch @ noon for 4 hands-on workshops using version control with git and GitHub.

**This week**

Collaborating in GitHub with Madison Swain-Bowden, Pamela Moriarty, Katie Jolly

**Content**

Collaborate with others on GitHub by working in the same repository and resolving version conflicts that can result. This content is based on the Carpentries 'Version control with git' (http://swcarpentry.github.io/git-novice/)

All workshop materials can be found at https://github.com/kdillmcfarland/2021-04-02-r-ladies-seattle-online/tree/gh-pages/_episodes/R-ladies

**Audience**

This workshop is meant for novice git users with some git experience, such as sessions 1-2 of this series. Priority registration will be given to those 1) attending all 4 sessions and 2) local Washington R-Ladies members.

We will be working in command line so those without experience may wish to review basic Unix commands (recommend lessons 1-3 at http://swcarpentry.github.io/shell-novice/)

**Prior to the workshop**

To prepare for this session:
- Install git https://git-scm.com/downloads
- Make a GitHub account https://github.com/

Or to prepare for all sessions:
- Install git https://git-scm.com/downloads
- Make a GitHub account https://github.com/
- Install GitHub Desktop https://desktop.github.com/
- Install R https://www.r-project.org/
- Install RStudio (free) https://rstudio.com/products/rstudio/download/

If you have issues with install, an instructor will be on the event Zoom 30 minutes prior to the workshop

title: git through April: GitHub GUI and RStudio
description:
Join us over lunch @ noon for 4 hands-on workshops using version control with git and GitHub.

**This week**

GitHub graphical user interface (GUI) and RStudio with Kim Dill-McFarland, Isabella Velasquez

**Content**

Avoid (most of) the command line hassle and use git/GitHub through a GUI or RStudio, including making repositories, tracking files, reviewing versions, and moving to/from GitHub. Some content is based on the Carpentries 'Version control with git' (http://swcarpentry.github.io/git-novice/)

All workshop materials can be found at https://github.com/kdillmcfarland/2021-04-02-r-ladies-seattle-online/tree/gh-pages/_episodes/R-ladies

**Audience**

This workshop is meant for novice git users and no prior experience is required. Priority registration will be given to those 1) attending all 4 sessions and 2) local Washington R-Ladies members.

**Prior to the workshop**

- Install git https://git-scm.com/downloads
- Make a GitHub account https://github.com/
- Install GitHub Desktop https://desktop.github.com/
- Install R https://www.r-project.org/
- Install RStudio (free) https://rstudio.com/products/rstudio/download/

If you have issues with install, an instructor will be on the event Zoom 30 minutes prior to the workshop

title: How to Give a Good Conference or Meetup Talk
description:
Join us for a discussion about coming up with, designing, and delivering a good talk!

Goals:
Help R-Ladies see themselves as experts and speakers
Demystify the process of applying to speak at conferences
Provide tips and resources for speaking at different types of events and formats
Alert R-Ladies Seattle members and others to upcoming opportunities
Have more people speak at R-Ladies!

Guests:
Dr. Kate Hertweck, Bioinformatics Training Manager at Fred Hutch
Dr. Marie Vendettuoli, Senior Statistical Programmer at Fred Hutch
Kara Woo, Kara Woo, Senior Data Science Engineer at InsightRX

Tech support:
Monica Gerber, R-Ladies Seattle Co-Organizer
Katie Jolly, R-Ladies Seattle Co-Organizer

Moderator:
Isabella Velásquez, R-Ladies Seattle Co-Organizer

title: Writing Presentations in R
description:
Come learn how to make beautiful slides with {xaringan} and deploy them! Silvia Canelón and Katie Jolly will be presenting.

If you're new to R-Ladies Seattle, please review the R-Ladies code of conduct:

The leadership, mentoring & teaching roles within this Community are held exclusively by minority genders (majority gender speakers may be allowed/invited as one-off guests in exceptional circumstances at the leadership team's discretion). Due to unexpected demand, we have opened learning participation to all genders, dependent on initial and on-going vetting by the leadership team. However, the stated priority of the R-Ladies communities is the development & support specifically of those identifying as a minority gender, and we, therefore, reserve the right to guard this interest through whatever measures the leadership team deem appropriate. Anyone involved with R-Ladies Seattle is expected to fully respect each other, the mandate of this community, and the goodwill on which R-Ladies is founded, or face expulsion/a penalty of any form, at the discretion of the leadership team. Full community guidelines are found here: https://github.com/rladies/starter-kit/wiki ...

title: Tutorial: Introduction to Parallel Processing in R
description:
Please join us for a lunchtime R-Ladies meetup with Brittany Barker. Brittany will guide us through a beginner-level tutorial on how and why to use parallel computing in R.

Brittany is a Research Associate (Postdoc) at the Oregon IPM Center at Oregon State University, where she is developing a spatial modeling platform (written in R) that predicts phenology and climate suitability of invasive insect species in real-time. She is broadly interested in how environmental perturbations such as climate change shape the population dynamics and geographical range limits of plant and animal species.

In her demo, she will demonstrate the use and value of the parallel, doParallel, and foreach packages for parallel computing in R. The demo will center on analyzing very large spatial data sets, but parallel processing in R can be used for ANY type of data. She will provide examples of when you may want to use parallel computing for your work, and provide further resources to explore.

This demo will be held over zoom and we will RStudio Cloud so you can follow along with the Brittany's code. We will email out instructions on what to expect and how to get set up.

~~How to follow Brittany’s tutorial:~~

**Recommended** Option 1: Create a free RStudio Cloud account and join Brittany’s shared workspace using the link below. This will create a copy of Brittany’s R project for you and allow to run her code. The advantage of using RStudio Cloud is that all of the packages you will need are pre-installed and you’ll be working from the same settings as Brittany. This will make it much easier for us to help you if you run into any problems.
https://rstudio.cloud/spaces/119389/join?access_code=412mjNHj6%2BJmLjN24nM8Px6UDtq7qILJh%2B064Io2

Option 2: If you don’t want to use RStudio Cloud, you can clone Brittany’s repo. You will need to install the R packages ahead of time. (Note: you will not be able to run the entire RMD file on your own computer unless you are using a Unix based OS.)
https://github.com/bbarker505/parallel_demo

If you want to watch the presentation and not run the code in real time, that is also fine.

Please reach out if you have any questions (email: seattle@rladies.org).

title: Reproducible Research
description:
**This event will be recorded!**

Join us over lunch for 3 talks on reproducible research/workflows!

Kim Dill-McFarland

Version control with git and GitHub-- An introduction to git tools to help you collaborate across space and time

Tracy Fuentes

How reproducible code is helping me learn to make clearer,
more compelling figures

Li-Ying Wang

Writing research papers with reproducible analysis using R Markdown compendia

Our host requires full covid-19 vaccination proof so please bring your vaccination card or a photo of it.

title: Introduction to R (virtual)
description:
Join the Seattle R-Ladies in this 1-hour, virtual workshop where we introduce you to R and RStudio at the beginner level. In it, we cover:

* R and RStudio including projects, scripts, and packages
* The help function
* Reading in data as a data frame and RData
* Data types

**Pre-requisites**
You **do not** need any prior experience in R or coding!

**Materials**
[https://bigslu.github.io/workshops/introR.workshop/introR.html](https://bigslu.github.io/workshops/introR.workshop/introR.html)

**In-person social**
Those in the Seattle area are invited to join us at our end of summer social after the workshop! Please RSVP to this event separately on Meetup at [https://www.meetup.com/rladies-seattle/events/288438564/](https://www.meetup.com/rladies-seattle/events/288438564/)

title: Reactor x PNW Tech Community Networking
description:
Join us at a networking event hosted by the Microsoft Reactor and partners - the PNW chapters of Seattle PyLadies, R-Ladies Seattle, and Data Circles. This is an opportunity to meet folks and hear from a few incredible community groups.

Register here or on the Reactor's Meetup page: https://www.meetup.com/Microsoft-Reactor-Redmond/events/282700053

Who is it aimed at?
If you are a part of the tech community or if you are interested in meeting people in the world of tech, this is an event you won’t want to miss.

Why should I attend?
If you miss connecting with your peers, join us to learn about fantastic community groups and meet industry professionals.

More about the groups we'll be joining!
PyLadies:
Seattle PyLadies is a Python programming group that welcomes members from all over the Puget Sound region. We started the Seattle chapter in 2012 and have grown to > 2000 members. PyLadies is an international mentorship group with a focus on helping more women become active participants and leaders in the Python open-source community. Our mission is to promote, educate and advance a diverse Python community through outreach, education, conferences, events and social gatherings.

Data Circles:
Data Circles started as Sea-WiDS (Seattle Women in Data Science). Data Circles exists to provide a place for women in data science to connect, build community, support each other and grow. Although we focus on empowerment of women, everybody is welcome to our events. We offer technical talks, workshops, networking opportunities, interview preparation sessions and more. Learn more about us at datacircles.org and, by joining the community you will enter our Slack space and connect to everyone in the community.

title: Highlights of posit::conf(2023)
description:
Join R-Ladies and useR Seattle for this joint event after posit::conf(2023). We will stream select talks from posit::conf and have a lively discussion among the attendees. If you have a specific talk you would like to see, please let use know by messaging the organizer here on Meetup or through our [Slack](https://pmyc0eskne.execute-api.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/Express/). You can find the posit::conf schedule at [https://posit.co/blog/keynotes-and-talks-at-posit-conf-2023/](https://posit.co/blog/keynotes-and-talks-at-posit-conf-2023/)

We will be hosted by the Fred Hutch Data Science Lab on the Fred Hutch Research Center Campus in the Arnold Building.

Afterwards, anyone over 21 is welcome to join us to go to an after-meetup beverage at a local bar. This is not covered by the event.

title: R-Ladies Social / Post-Cascadia R Conference
description:
Join us for a social after Cascadia R Conference!

Cascadia R Conference is an R conference serving the Pacific Northwest (OR/WA/BC). This event is a place to come together around R and get together with like-minded people across the region. Interested? [Learn more on their website and register today](https://cascadiarconf.com/)!

The conference concludes at 6 pm on August 19th. Afterwards, we invite R-Ladies to join us at a social gathering. Come even if you didn't attend the conference. It's a great time to meet and chat with other R enthusiasts from the area!

We will share the location closer to the event. Please note, each member will be responsible for covering their own food expenses. We appreciate your understanding and hope to see you there.

title: Winter Social
description:
Let's gather for some fun in this chilly weather - join us at the upcoming R-Ladies Seattle winter social! It's the perfect opportunity to meet other R-Ladies and bond over your shared love for R programming.

We're meeting at the TeKu Tavern, a cozy bar that boasts a wide variety of global brews and non-alcoholic beverages. Show off your spiRit by collecting awesome hex stickers and participating in our R-themed raffle.

Please note that the event is for R-Ladies members only. It is limited to 24 RSVPs due to space constraints, so please update your response if your plans change.

We can't wait to see you and celebrate the winter season together!

Chips and salsa provided!

Non-alcoholic and alcoholic beverages available. Attendees are responsible for their own tab.

title: Swashbucklin' Data Science: Navigatin' R fer Talk Like a Pirate Day!
description:
[Workshop GitHub](https://github.com/MLWorkshops/swashbuckling-r)

🏴‍☠️ Ahoy, useRRRRRs! 🏴‍☠️

Join Seattle R-Ladies and Seattle AI Workshop Meetups for a swashbuckling evening of data exploration at our R Pirate Day event. Whether ye be a landlubber new to R or a seasoned data buccaneeR, there’s something for everyone! Set sail with us on September 18 from 5-8pm at the Microsoft Redmond Reactor for an evening filled with deep dives into data science, networkin’, grub, jolly fun and plenty of pirate puns.

**Speakers and Workshops**:

* **Micheleen Harris**: Get Hooked on ggplot: craftin’ stunning graphics on the high seas
* **Kim Dill-McFarland**: Rough tides: How to improve your big data visualizations in R
* **Simran Saxena**: Aye-Eye NLP: Digging deep for emotional treasure

**Event Details:**

* **Location**: Microsoft Redmond Reactor (3709 157th Ave NE, Redmond, WA 98052)
* **Date & Time**: September 18, 2024, 5:00 PM - 8:00 PM ⏰
* **Food**: Pizza, salad, and drinks courtesy of our sponsor, Microsoft Reactor
* **Prerequisites**: Beginner knowledge of R and RStudio including loading and manipulating data frames, base plots, and saving outputs. Completely new to R? Check the resources below.

**Workshop Resources:**

* Check out our GitHub organization for workshop materials:[ MLWorkshops GitHub](https://github.com/MLWorkshops)
* Set up your RStudio server with this Docker setup:[ ](https://github.com/dataqueso/docker-rstudio-server-pop-genetics)[coming soon]
* Prepare in advance with Introduction to R from Kim:[ Workshop Resources](https://bigslu.github.io/workshops/)

Don’t miss this opportunity to enhance your R skills and network with fellow data enthusiasts. Pirate attire is optional but highly encouraged! Arrr you ready?

title: International Women’s Day 2024 | Impact the Future
description:
To all women in tech, come and celebrate with us the International Women's Day meetup PARTY! A collaboration of data science meetups in the Greater Seattle area.

To all our allies, this is a women event and is inclusive and everyone is welcome to attend.

Our vision is to build economic opportunity for women through technology. Our programs provide visibility, community, and resources for women in technology.

We all have the power and the responsibility to impact the future of technology and society, with AI as a major part of shaping our world. What will that future look like? And how will we build it? We can use our skills, creativity and passion to create positive change and solve the problems that matter to us and our communities. We can also inspire the next generation of women in tech who will continue to impact the future with their own ideas and innovations.

An event for our local tech community, with a strong focus on knowledge exchange, networking, and learning. Get together with [RLadies Seattle](https://www.meetup.com/rladies-seattle/), [Seattle Spark + AI](https://www.linkedin.com/company/seattle-spark-ai/), [PyLadies Seattle](https://www.linkedin.com/company/pyladies-seattle/), [GDG Bellevue](https://www.linkedin.com/company/gdg-bellevue), [PyData Seattle](https://www.linkedin.com/company/pydata-seattle/), and [Women Techmakers Seattle](https://www.linkedin.com/company/wtmseattle), [Seattle Artificial Intelligence Workshops](https://www.meetup.com/seattle-artificial-intelligence-workshops/)

Event program:

* 6:00 - 6:30 - Candy 🍭🍬 eat, beverages, swag and network - Donated by [Databricks](https://www.databricks.com/) and [Delta Lake](https://delta.io/) 🍿🍔 Hamburguers, Fried Chicken, Tamales, Cuban Sandwich!
* 6:30 - 6:40 - Announcements
* 6:40 - 7:10 - Women Speaker 1, 2, 3
* 7:10 - 7:40 - Women Speaker 4, 5, 6
* 7:40 - 8:20 - Networking + Pie bar! 🍭🍰🧁

We recommend to arrive early as seats are limited and are first come first serve.

2 Parking options:

* There is pay parking at the building.
* [Bellevue Place and Lincoln Square parking](https://bellevuecollection.com/visit-here/parking/) is complimentary with validation from Bellevue Place and Lincoln Square retail, restaurants and entertainment. After 8:00 p.m. – No validation is necessary.

title: R-Ladies Monthly Happy Hour
description:
NOTE: This month our Happy Hour is one week earlier than normal. It will be on the first Tuesday, March 5.

Join R-Ladies Seattle for our monthly happy hour! Come and network with local R-Ladies to make new connections and discuss all things R. This is a casual event so you can stop by any time. You'll find us by our logo (and probably Kim's rainbow hair).

Chips and salsa provided!

Non-alcoholic and alcoholic beverages available. Attendees are responsible for their own tab.

title: Beautifying R Outputs: A Mentor/Mentee Event
description:
Bring your code and join us for a collaborative exploration to beautify your R outputs. Whether you're a mentee seeking guidance or a mentor eager to share your expertise, this event provides a platform to enhance your R skills! This will be a collaborative and inclusive environment where participants can learn, share, and grow together.

This event encourages mentorship and collaboration. Attendees are invited to bring their code and seek guidance from experienced individuals in packages related to data visualization and tables including **gt, ggplot, quarto, plotly, highcharter, officeverse, and shiny**. (Please be sure this code can be shared with others!)

If you're interested in volunteering as a mentor or have specific topics you'd like covered, please reach out to the organizers on Meetup.

After the event, join us for a casual social gathering at a local venue. Note that beverages are not covered by the event.

We look forward to seeing you there! Please come to the entrance of the main ARNOLD BUILDING.

If you are **not an R-Ladies member**, please RSVP in the useR event [https://www.meetup.com/seattle-user/](https://www.meetup.com/seattle-user/)

title: 💜 From R to Python to R 💜
description:
What happens when #Rstats and #Python collide for an evening of learning, laughter, and connection? Let’s find out together! 🎉

Join us as Courtney shares her journey through the challenges and triumphs of analyzing single-cell sequencing data. Beginning with her go-to tool R, she faced difficulties managing data from nearly a million cells. This led her to Python, discovering new strategies and gaining valuable insights. Ultimately, she then returned to R, leveraging her expertise for the final stages of data visualization. This talk highlights the interplay between R and Python, showcasing lessons learned and practical tips for R coders learning python and python coders learning R.

But that’s not all – there will be games, plenty of pizza 🍕, and lots of chances to connect with fellow enthusiasts. Don’t miss the fun at the Microsoft Reactor in Redmond brought to you by **R-Ladies Seattle, PyLadies Seattle, and Seattle AI Workshops**. See you there! 🚀

**Speaker Bio**
Courtney Armour PhD is a Bioinformatician at the U of Washington, School of Medicine. She uses her computational expertise to analyze changes in gene expression associated with asthma and kidney disease. She is passionate about reproducible workflows, data visualization, and helping others learn to code. When not at the computer, Courtney enjoys going on adventures with her high-energy German short-haired pointer.
